content:
Using machine learning to spot hidden behavior patterns while keeping human empathy at the center of design decisions.

23 min read
Just now

--

I remember sitting in a research review meeting where a product team presented findings generated entirely by an AI summarization tool. The slides were beautiful. The insights were neatly packaged into digestible bullet points. The only problem was that the core conclusion was entirely wrong.
The AI had analyzed the transcripts of twenty user interviews and concluded that users wanted a more complex dashboard with deeper analytics. The human researchers, eager to save time and move quickly, took the summary and ran with it. They designed a feature based on this AI generated insight.
Before they started building, I pulled up the raw transcript of one interview that the AI had cited as primary evidence. The user had spent ten minutes complaining about how overwhelming the current dashboard was. At one point, they said something like, “I guess I just want to see all the data so I can figure it out myself.” The AI flagged this phrase and categorized it under a desire for more data and complexity.
But if you listened to the audio, the user was sighing heavily. They were frustrated. They were being sarcastic. They did not want more data. They wanted the existing data organized better so they did not have to do the mental heavy lifting.
This moment captured the core challenge we face today. We have access to incredible machine learning tools that can process thousands of data points in seconds. But in our rush to automate the hard parts of our jobs, we are forgetting a fundamental truth. Data processing is not the same as understanding.
The bottleneck in user research has shifted. It is no longer about gathering data or even sorting data. It is about interpreting meaning. Machine learning is incredible at sorting, clustering, and finding statistical patterns. It is terrible at understanding context, emotion, and unspoken human frustration. Meaning requires lived experience. It requires understanding the sighs, the hesitations, and the cultural context that an algorithm cannot see.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image by: Google Nano BananaThe Shift From Data Scarcity to Insight Scarcity
Ten years ago, the biggest challenge in UX research was getting enough data. We begged for budget to run usability tests. We struggled to recruit the right participants. We spent weeks conducting enough interviews to feel confident in our findings.
Today, the problem is entirely different. We are drowning in data. Every click, every scroll, every support ticket is tracked. We have session recordings, heatmaps, product analytics, and endless interview transcripts. The scarcity is no longer in data. The scarcity is in insight.
When you have ten thousand user feedback tickets, you cannot read them all manually. This is where machine learning steps in and provides genuine value. Topic modeling algorithms can cluster those ten thousand tickets into ten thematic groups in seconds. Natural language processing can tell you which features are mentioned most frequently.
But knowing that login issues are the most frequent topic does not tell you why they are happening or how users feel about them. The machine gives you the map. It does not tell you where to dig for gold.
Many product teams misunderstand this. They see a beautifully clustered chart and treat it as the final answer. They build roadmaps based on frequency of mentions rather than depth of problem. If an AI tells them users mention dark mode five hundred times, they assume dark mode is a high priority. But the AI cannot tell them that four hundred of those mentions were from a single vocal thread of power users who do not represent the broader customer base.
The researcher’s job is no longer just to find the data. The researcher’s job is to interrogate the data, even when, or especially when, it is presented by an algorithm.
Why Machine Learning Struggles With Human Sarcasm
Algorithms are literal. They are trained on text. They do not understand tone of voice, body language, or the shared cultural context that makes human communication work.
In user interviews, people rarely say exactly what they mean. They are polite. They hedge. They use sarcasm. They tell you what they think you want to hear.
A user might say, “Oh sure, I read the instructions. They were super helpful.” A basic sentiment analysis model will flag this as highly positive. It sees the words super helpful and assigns a positive sentiment score.
A human researcher watching the video sees the user rolling their eyes. They hear the flat tone of voice. They know the user did not read the instructions and found them completely useless.
This is the limitation of natural language processing in qualitative research. Language is a poor proxy for thought. We say things to navigate social situations. In a research setting, users want to please the moderator. They want to sound smart. They do not want to admit they were confused by a simple interface.
Machine learning models assume that the text is an accurate representation of the user’s internal state. Human researchers know that the text is often a performance. The real insight lives in the gap between what the user says and what they actually do.
When we rely too heavily on AI summaries, we lose the ability to read between the lines. We take the text at face value. We miss the eye rolls, the pauses, and the hesitations. We build products for the literal words users say, rather than the underlying problems they are trying to solve.
The Illusion of Objectivity in Algorithmic Clustering
One of the most dangerous aspects of AI in research is the illusion of objectivity. When a human researcher codes qualitative data, they acknowledge their biases. They keep reflexivity journals. They double code with a peer to ensure their interpretations are not purely subjective.
When an AI clusters data, it presents itself as objective. It is just math, after all. But the math is based on decisions made by the model creators. The way an algorithm groups text reflects its underlying parameters, which are often opaque to the end user.
If you run a topic modeling algorithm on your interview transcripts, the model decides how many topics exist and what words belong to each topic. It might group pricing and value together. It might separate them. These grouping decisions fundamentally shape how you view the data.
I have seen teams accept algorithmic clusters without question because the output looks like a clean, objective truth. A dendrogram or a scatter plot feels scientific. It feels rigorous. But an algorithm can only find patterns based on the structural properties of the text. It cannot understand semantic meaning in a deep way.
Two users might use completely different words to describe the exact same frustration. One might say, “The app keeps logging me out.” Another might say, “I have to sign in every time I open it.” A human researcher instantly knows these are the same issue. A basic clustering algorithm might put them in separate clusters because the vocabulary is different.
The reverse is also true. Two users might use the same word to describe completely different experiences. A human reading the context knows the difference. An algorithm counting frequencies does not.
The lesson here is not that we should abandon algorithmic clustering. The lesson is that we must treat AI outputs as hypotheses to be tested, not as objective truths. When the AI groups data together, we must ask why. We must go back to the raw data and verify that the grouping makes human sense.
The Synthetic User Trap and The Death of Novelty
Recently, a new trend has emerged in the design world. Teams are using generative AI to create synthetic users. The idea is that you prompt an AI to act like a persona. You tell it to be a thirty five year old project manager who uses Jira and struggles with work life balance. Then you interview the AI to get rapid design feedback.
This sounds incredibly efficient. You do not need to recruit participants. You do not need to schedule interviews. You can run a hundred synthetic interviews in an afternoon.
But this practice is fundamentally flawed. It confuses a statistical average with a real human being.
Synthetic users are trained on existing data. When you ask a synthetic user a question, it generates a response based on what the average thirty five year old project manager might say based on internet training data. It gives you the expected answer.
Research is not about finding the expected answer. Research is about discovering the unexpected. It is about finding the edge cases, the unmet needs, and the workarounds that people invent when your product fails them.
You cannot discover the unknown by asking a machine to hallucinate based on the known. If you only talk to synthetic users, you will only design for the average. You will miss the people who use your product in weird, creative, and frustrating ways.
Erika Hall, in her book Just Enough Research, reminds us that the goal of research is to reduce uncertainty. Synthetic users do the opposite. They increase your confidence in your existing assumptions. They reflect your biases back to you. If you assume project managers care mostly about reporting, your synthetic user will likely confirm that assumption because the training data associates project managers with reporting.
Real users surprise you. They tell you they do not use your most prized feature. They tell you they hacked your product to do something it was never designed to do. They tell you they pay for your software just to access one tiny button hidden in the settings menu.
Synthetic users will never give you these insights. They are mirrors, not windows. Use them for brainstorming if you want, but never use them as a substitute for actual user research.
The Danger of Feature Frequency as Priority
In many product organizations, roadmaps are built based on the loudest voices. Historically, the loudest voices were the sales team complaining about lost deals, or the CEO who had a random idea in the shower. Today, the loudest voice is often an AI generated frequency chart.
When product teams use AI to analyze feedback forms, support tickets, and app store reviews, the default output is a list of features ranked by mention frequency. It is incredibly easy to take that list, turn it into a presentation, and say the data shows we need to build a certain feature because it was mentioned four hundred times.
This approach is deeply flawed. Frequency of mention is not a proxy for value.
Imagine you run a productivity app. Users frequently request a dark mode. They also frequently mention that the app crashes when they try to sync large files. Both issues might get mentioned four hundred times. An AI frequency chart treats them as equal priorities.
But a human researcher knows better. Dark mode is a nice to have. It improves the experience for a subset of users. The sync crash is a critical failure. It destroys trust and causes data loss. If you only look at the frequency chart, you might prioritize dark mode because it is easier to build and will satisfy four hundred mentions quickly.
The researcher’s job is to weight the data. Not all feedback is created equal. A user mentioning a bug that deletes their work is expressing a different level of need than a user wishing the app had a different color scheme. AI tools struggle with this weighting. They count words. They do not understand the severity of the underlying problem.
I worked with a team that was obsessed with their AI driven feedback dashboard. They had a Top Requested Features list that updated in real time. The engineering team would pick items off the top of the list and build them. They felt incredibly productive. They were shipping features at a record pace.
But their retention numbers were dropping. The reason wa
